subUnits.title: 1.1 Speech: Selected Consonant Sounds /j/, /z/, /ʒ/
notes: • Examples: view, yam, beauty, yet, young, yard
• Pronunciation: Tongue tip touches alveolar ridge, then releases with friction
• Practice: Repeat after teacher - 'Yes, I view the beautiful yam'
• Nigerian context: Common in Nigerian English pronunciation of words like 'yam', 'youth'
• Examples: beds, goes, buzz, zoo, zero, please
• Pronunciation: Tongue tip near alveolar ridge, voiced vibration
• Practice: 'The bees buzz around the roses'
• Nigerian context: Distinguish from /s/ sound - 'zoo' vs 'soo'
• Examples: vision, television, occasion, measure, pleasure
• Pronunciation: Tongue blade near palate, voiced vibration
• Nigerian context: Often confused with /ʃ/ - 'vision' vs 'fission'
• Listen carefully to teacher's model pronunciation
• Record yourself and compare with correct pronunciation
diagramDescriptions: Articulation diagram showing tongue position for /j/, /z/, /ʒ/ sounds
Chart of words containing each consonant sound
Nigerian context: Common words with these sounds in Nigerian English
subUnits.youtubeQuery: English consonant sounds /j/ /z/ /ʒ/ pronunciation SS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.q: Which consonant sound is found in the word 'yam'?
quiz.explanation: The word 'yam' contains the consonant sound /j/, which is a voiced palato-alveolar affricate.

quiz.q: The consonant sound /z/ is found in which of the following words?
quiz.explanation: The word 'buzz' contains the consonant sound /z/, which is a voiced alveolar fricative.

quiz.q: Which sound is often confused with /ʒ/ in Nigerian English?
quiz.explanation: The sound /ʒ/ (as in 'vision') is often confused with /ʃ/ (as in 'fission') in Nigerian English pronunciation.

quiz.q: The word 'television' contains which consonant sound?
quiz.explanation: The word 'television' contains the consonant sound /ʒ/ in the middle of the word.

quiz.q: Which of the following words contains the /z/ sound?
quiz.explanation: The word 'please' contains the consonant sound /z/ at the end.

quiz.q: The consonant sound /j/ is a:
quiz.explanation: The consonant sound /j/ is a voiced palato-alveolar affricate, produced with the tongue tip touching the alveolar ridge.

quiz.q: Which practice method is recommended for mastering these consonant sounds?
opts: Repeating words after the teacher and using them in sentences
quiz.explanation: The recommended practice method is to repeat words after the teacher and use them in sentences for better pronunciation.

quiz.q: The word 'measure' contains which consonant sound?
quiz.explanation: The word 'measure' contains the consonant sound /ʒ/ in the middle.

quiz.q: In Nigerian English, the word 'zero' is pronounced with which sound?
quiz.explanation: The word 'zero' in Nigerian English is pronounced with the consonant sound /z/.

quiz.q: Which of the following is NOT an example of words with /ʒ/ sound?
quiz.explanation: The word 'yam' contains the /j/ sound, not the /ʒ/ sound. Vision, television, and occasion all contain /ʒ/.

subUnits.title: 1.2 Vocabulary: Words Associated with Home and Family
notes: • Nuclear family: father, mother, son, daughter, sibling
• Extended family: uncle, aunt, cousin, nephew, niece
• Nigerian context: Extended family system is central to Nigerian culture
• Rooms: parlour, bedroom, kitchen, bathroom, dining room, store
• Furniture: chair, table, bed, cupboard, sofa, wardrobe
• Kitchen utensils: pot, pan, spoon, fork, knife, plate, bowl
• Nigerian context: Traditional homes may have compound, veranda, outdoor kitchen
• Definition: Names of general people, places, or things
• Examples: country, city, man, river, festival, house, car
• Not capitalized unless beginning a sentence
• Definition: Names of specific people, places, or things
• Examples: Nigeria, Lagos, Mr. Ade, River Niger, Christmas
• Nigerian context: Names of Nigerian states, cities, festivals
• Capitalize names of people: Chinedu, Aisha, Ibrahim
• Capitalize names of places: Abuja, Kano, Port Harcourt
• Capitalize names of institutions: University of Lagos, NNPC
• Capitalize days, months, festivals: Monday, December, Eid-el-Kabir
• Nigerian context: Proper capitalization shows respect and clarity
diagramDescriptions: Family tree diagram illustrating nuclear and extended family terms
Chart of home environment vocabulary: rooms, furniture, utensils
Comparison of common nouns vs proper nouns with Nigerian examples
Capital letter usage rules with examples
subUnits.youtubeQuery: English vocabulary home family nouns SS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.q: Which of the following is a proper noun?
quiz.explanation: 'Lagos' is a proper noun because it names a specific place. 'City', 'river', and 'festival' are common nouns.

quiz.q: Which of the following is an example of a common noun?
quiz.explanation: 'Country' is a common noun because it names a general thing. 'Nigeria', 'Mr. Ade', and 'Christmas' are proper nouns.

quiz.q: Which word should be capitalized?
quiz.explanation: 'Abuja' should be capitalized because it is a proper noun (name of a specific place). 'Uncle', 'aunt', and 'cousin' are common nouns.

quiz.q: In Nigerian culture, which family system is central?
quiz.explanation: The extended family system is central to Nigerian culture, including uncles, aunts, cousins, and other relatives.

quiz.q: Which of the following is a kitchen utensil?
quiz.explanation: 'Spoon' is a kitchen utensil. 'Bed', 'sofa', and 'wardrobe' are furniture items.

quiz.q: Which of the following should be capitalized?
quiz.explanation: All of the above should be capitalized: 'Monday' (day), 'December' (month), and 'Eid-el-Kabir' (festival) are proper nouns.

quiz.q: Which of the following is NOT a room in a house?
quiz.explanation: 'Compound' is an outdoor area, not a room. 'Parlour', 'kitchen', and 'bathroom' are rooms in a house.

quiz.q: The word 'father-in-law' refers to:
quiz.explanation: 'Father-in-law' refers to your spouse's father, which is a relationship by marriage (in-law).

quiz.q: Which of the following is a proper noun in Nigerian context?
quiz.explanation: 'University of Lagos' is a proper noun because it names a specific institution. 'University', 'school', and 'college' are common nouns.

quiz.q: In traditional Nigerian homes, which feature might be found?
quiz.explanation: Traditional Nigerian homes may have compound and veranda, which are outdoor spaces common in Nigerian architecture.

subUnits.title: 1.3 Structures: Nouns – Types of Nouns
notes: • A noun is a word that names a person, place, thing, or idea
• Names general people, places, or things
• Not capitalized unless beginning a sentence
• Examples: country, city, man, river, festival
• Names specific people, places, or things
• Examples: Nigeria, Lagos, Mr. Ade, River Niger, Christmas
• Nigerian context: Names of Nigerian states, cities, festivals
• Names things that can be perceived by the senses
• Can be seen, touched, heard, smelled, or tasted
• Names ideas, qualities, or states that cannot be touched
• Cannot be perceived by the senses
• Names groups of people or things
• Examples: team, family, flock, committee, class
• Nigerian context: 'A crowd of worshippers', 'a troupe of dancers'
• Nigerian context: 'a cup of water', 'two cups of sugar'
diagramDescriptions: Chart of noun types with Nigerian examples
Comparison of concrete vs abstract nouns
Examples of collective nouns in Nigerian context
Countable vs uncountable nouns with examples
subUnits.youtubeQuery: English grammar types of nouns SS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.q: Which type of noun names a specific person, place, or thing?
quiz.explanation: A proper noun names a specific person, place, or thing and is always capitalized, e.g., 'Nigeria', 'Lagos'.

quiz.q: Which of the following is an abstract noun?
quiz.explanation: 'Honesty' is an abstract noun because it names a quality that cannot be perceived by the senses. 'Table', 'water', and 'music' are concrete nouns.

quiz.q: Which of the following is a collective noun?
quiz.explanation: 'Team' is a collective noun because it names a group of people. 'Boy' is a common noun, 'Lagos' is a proper noun, and 'freedom' is an abstract noun.

quiz.q: Which type of noun can be perceived by the senses?
quiz.explanation: Concrete nouns name things that can be perceived by the senses (seen, touched, heard, smelled, or tasted).

quiz.q: Which of the following is an uncountable noun?
quiz.explanation: 'Water' is an uncountable noun because it cannot be counted. 'Book', 'child', and 'city' are countable nouns.

quiz.q: In Nigerian context, which is a proper noun?
quiz.explanation: 'River Niger' is a proper noun because it names a specific river. 'River', 'festival', and 'city' are common nouns.

quiz.q: Which of the following is a countable noun?
quiz.explanation: 'Book' is a countable noun because it can be counted (one book, two books). 'Rice', 'information', and 'advice' are uncountable.

quiz.q: A noun that names an idea or quality is called:
quiz.explanation: An abstract noun names ideas, qualities, or states that cannot be touched or perceived by the senses, e.g., 'love', 'freedom'.

quiz.q: Which of the following is NOT a type of noun?
quiz.explanation: 'Adjective noun' is not a type of noun. The main types are common, proper, concrete, abstract, collective, countable, and uncountable nouns.

quiz.q: In Nigerian context, 'a crowd of worshippers' is an example of:
quiz.explanation: 'A crowd of worshippers' is a collective noun because it names a group of people.

subUnits.title: 1.4 Comprehension: Reading to Comprehend Main or Story Line
notes: • Reading to understand the main idea or story line
• Identifying key points and supporting details
• What is the passage mainly about?
• What is the central theme or message?
• Facts that support the main idea
4. Answer questions based on the text
• Use context clues for word meanings
• Restate the main idea in your own words
• Passages may reflect Nigerian culture, values, and social issues
• Examples: family life, education, community development, national unity
diagramDescriptions: Steps for effective comprehension with examples
Diagram showing main idea vs supporting details
Chart of comprehension skills: identifying, inferring, summarizing
Nigerian context: Sample passage on home life with comprehension questions
subUnits.youtubeQuery: English comprehension reading skills SS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.q: What is the first step in effective comprehension?
quiz.explanation: The first step in effective comprehension is to read the passage carefully, noting unfamiliar words and underlining key points.

quiz.q: What is the main idea of a passage?
quiz.explanation: The main idea is the central theme or message of the passage - what the passage is mainly about.

quiz.q: Which skill involves distinguishing fact from opinion?
quiz.explanation: Comprehension skills include distinguishing fact from opinion, identifying topic sentences, and making inferences.

quiz.q: What are supporting details?
opts: Facts that support the main idea
quiz.explanation: Supporting details are facts, examples, and explanations that support the main idea of the passage.

quiz.q: What should you do with unfamiliar words in a passage?
opts: Note them and use context clues
quiz.explanation: You should note unfamiliar words and use context clues to determine their meanings while reading.

quiz.q: What is a topic sentence?
opts: A sentence that states the main idea of a paragraph
quiz.explanation: A topic sentence is a sentence that states the main idea of a paragraph, usually found at the beginning.

quiz.q: In Nigerian context, comprehension passages may reflect:
opts: Nigerian culture, values, and social issues
quiz.explanation: In Nigerian context, comprehension passages may reflect Nigerian culture, values, and social issues like family life, education, and community development.

quiz.q: What is the purpose of summarizing?
opts: To restate the main idea in your own words
quiz.explanation: The purpose of summarizing is to restate the main idea and important supporting details in your own words, keeping it brief and clear.

quiz.q: What are context clues?
opts: Hints in the text that help determine word meanings
quiz.explanation: Context clues are hints in the surrounding text that help you determine the meaning of unfamiliar words.

quiz.q: Which of the following is NOT a comprehension skill?
quiz.explanation: Pronouncing words correctly is a speech skill, not a comprehension skill. Comprehension skills include identifying topic sentences, making inferences, and summarizing.

subUnits.title: 1.5 Writing: Narrative Composition
notes: • A narrative essay tells a story, either real or imagined
• It recounts events in a logical, chronological order
• Uses descriptive language to create vivid images
• Middle: Development of plot through events
• Past simple: 'I went to school yesterday'
• Past continuous: 'I was walking when it started to rain'
• Past perfect: 'I had finished my homework before dinner'
• Use of adjectives to describe people, places, things
• Use of adverbs to describe actions
• Create vivid images for the reader
• Setting: time and place of the story
• Use of transitional words: first, then, suddenly, finally
• Reflection on the experience or lesson learned
• A day I will never forget
• The day I met an AIDS patient
• The day Chukwu was sentenced to prison for stealing a car
• Each paragraph should begin with a clear topic sentence
• Topic sentence states the main idea of the paragraph
• Supporting sentences provide details, examples, explanations
• Example: 'My first day at school was both exciting and frightening.'
• Compound sentences: Two independent clauses joined by conjunction
Example: 'The sun rose, and the birds began to sing.'
• Complex sentences: One independent clause + one or more dependent clauses
Example: 'When the sun rose, the birds began to sing.'
• Comma (,) separates items in a list or clauses
diagramDescriptions: Structure diagram of narrative essay: introduction, body, conclusion
Chart of tense forms for narrative essays
Examples of topic sentences for different paragraphs
Comparison of simple, compound, and complex sentences
Nigerian context: Sample narrative topics reflecting local experiences
subUnits.youtubeQuery: English narrative composition writing SS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.q: What is the main purpose of a narrative composition?
quiz.explanation: The main purpose of a narrative composition is to tell a story, either real or imagined, in a logical sequence.

quiz.q: Which tense is most commonly used in narrative essays?
quiz.explanation: Narrative essays typically use past tense to recount events that have already happened.

quiz.q: What should the introduction of a narrative essay include?
opts: Hook, setting, and introduction of characters
quiz.explanation: The introduction should include a hook to capture interest, the setting (time and place), and introduction of main characters.

quiz.q: Which of the following is a transitional word?
quiz.explanation: 'Then' is a transitional word used to show sequence of events. Other transitional words include 'first', 'suddenly', 'finally'.

quiz.q: What is a topic sentence?
opts: The last sentence of a paragraph
A sentence that states the main idea of a paragraph
quiz.explanation: A topic sentence states the main idea of a paragraph and is usually found at the beginning.

quiz.q: Which sentence type has one independent clause?
quiz.explanation: A simple sentence has one independent clause. Example: 'The sun rose.'

quiz.q: What punctuation mark encloses direct speech?
quiz.explanation: Quotation marks ("") are used to enclose direct speech or dialogue in writing.

quiz.q: Which of the following is NOT a feature of narrative composition?
quiz.explanation: Statistical data is not a feature of narrative composition. Narratives use clear sequence, past tense, descriptive language, and dialogue.

quiz.q: What should the conclusion of a narrative essay include?
quiz.explanation: The conclusion should include the resolution of the story and reflection on the experience or lesson learned.

quiz.q: Which of the following is an example of a compound sentence?
opts: The sun rose, and the birds began to sing.
When the sun rose, the birds began to sing.
quiz.explanation: 'The sun rose, and the birds began to sing.' is a compound sentence because it has two independent clauses joined by a conjunction.
